items.myth: Setting random_state=42 guarantees identical results on any machine.
items.reality: Seeds fix pseudo-random sequences for a given software stack, but different library versions, hardware (CPU vs GPU), and nondeterministic GPU kernels can still produce small or large metric drift.
items.example: Same seed on PyTorch 2.0 vs 2.2 may differ slightly; same seed on CPU vs default GPU training often differs more.

items.myth: Reproducibility is only about random seeds.
items.reality: Full reproducibility also requires versioned code, data, hyperparameters, and a pinned environment (Python, libraries, CUDA). Seeds control randomness; pinning controls runtime behavior.
items.example: Seed 0 with yesterday's dataset file and today's edited preprocessing script will not recreate last week's accuracy.

items.myth: You should always pick seed 0 or 42 for scientific validity.
items.reality: The numeric value of a seed is arbitrary — any fixed integer works. What matters is documenting it and holding it constant across comparable runs.
items.example: random_state=12345 is equally valid if logged and reused for every trial in a sweep.

items.myth: Deterministic mode has no downside on GPUs.
items.reality: Strict deterministic algorithms in PyTorch and similar frameworks can disable fast nondeterministic kernels, increasing training time or blocking certain ops entirely.
items.example: torch.backends.cudnn.benchmark = True speeds training but can introduce run-to-run variation even with a fixed seed.
